verified_matches: Excitatory C_m=0.5 nF, g_m=25 nS, tau_rp=2 ms
Inhibitory C_m=0.2 nF, g_m=20 nS, tau_rp=1 ms
theta=-50 mV, V_L=-70 mV, V_rst=-55 mV
NMDAR: beta=0.062 mV^-1, gamma=3.57 mM, [Mg2+]=1 mM
AMPA: tau_l=1 ms, tau_r=0.2 ms, tau_d=2 ms
NMDA: tau_l=1 ms, tau_r=2 ms, tau_d=100 ms
GABA: tau_l=1 ms, tau_r=0.5 ms, tau_d=5 ms
Baseline firing rates: 5.2/20 Hz (steady), 5.5/21 Hz (critical)
+5% input rates: 7.8/26 Hz (steady), 15/42 Hz (critical)
Simulated oscillation ~50 Hz, predicted onset 58 Hz, experimental ~55 Hz
Correlation peak lags: +/-18 ms experimental, +/-20 ms model
Contributing pairs: 978, 1,246, 368, 434
Phencyclidine 0.25-0.30 mg/kg IM
Electrodes 400 um spacing, 400-1,400 um range
AX 69% of trials, non-AX 31%
Cue 1,000 ms, delay 1,000 ms, probe 500 ms
Delta t = 0.1 ms simulation step
I_NMDA/I_GABA = 0.2; nu_X = 5 Hz external rate
nu_E=5 Hz, nu_I=20 Hz prescribed rates
Sliding 100 ms window, 2 ms timing resolution
Geometric mean firing rate threshold >=5 Hz, K~200 trials
Synchrony rise ~200 ms before motor response
unverified_but_plausible: 47 neural ensembles, 893 prefrontal neurons (from source paper context; not in extraction snippets)
Brodmann area 46, 16-electrode Thomas Recording drive (consistent with source paper)
correctness_details.notes: All numerical parameters and measurements in the paper match the extraction precisely, including synaptic time constants, neuron biophysics, firing rates under both baseline and +5% perturbation, oscillation frequencies, and correlation lag structure. No fabricated or inconsistent numbers detected.

writing_details.paragraph_structure_quality: excellent: every paragraph has a clear topic sentence, develops a single mechanistic point, and closes with a transitional sentence that sets up the next paragraph; the Results section reads as a linear argument rather than a list of findings
writing_details.transitions_quality: exemplary; connective tissue is explicit and semantically motivated ('Having established that...', 'This finding establishes a direct mechanistic parallel...', 'Beyond the quantitative agreement...'); paragraphs are causally linked

comparison_to_v1: Writing axis improved from 88 (v1) to 94 (v2). The v2 discussion now contains a sophisticated reconciliation with Compte et al. 2000 (the apparent paradox of NMDA reduction vs AMPA compensation) that was absent in v1, and the introduction more clearly scaffolds the mechanistic gap. Presentation rose from 92 to 93 with well-chosen tables for neuron/synaptic/NMDAR parameters and firing rates. Citations axis improved (90 vs 86) through more contextually precise placement. Figures axis improved (84 vs 80) with a pipeline-style network schematic, state diagram, firing rates panel, and model-vs-experiment comparison. Overall rose from 90 (v1) to 93 (v2), now exceeding the single-agent baseline (91).
brief_notes: This is the strongest paper in the series on nearly every axis. The mechanistic argument is tightly constructed: the experimental observations are summarized first, then the architecture, then mean field and stability analysis, then simulations with the +5% perturbation, then NMDAR reduction, then direct quantitative comparison. Topic sentences carry the argument without the reader needing to work. The discussion handles a genuine subtlety (NMDAR reduction with vs without AMPA compensation) that only a careful writer would flag. Correctness is essentially perfect: every biophysical parameter, firing rate, and frequency matches the extraction. Figures are still schematic/placeholder-quality rather than data-rich panels, which modestly caps that axis, but their captions accurately embed the relevant numbers. Clearly beats the single-agent baseline.
